examples.title: First Fit Allocation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to allocate memory using First Fit strategy
examples.explanation: The First Fit algorithm scans the free list from the beginning and allocates memory from the first block that is large enough. It is fast because it doesn't search for the best match, and tends to leave larger holes at the end of memory.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of free blocks

examples.title: Best Fit Allocation Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation of Best Fit memory allocation strategy
examples.code:
class MemoryBlock:
    def __init__(self, start, size, process_id=None):
        self.start = start
        self.size = size
        self.process_id = process_id
        
def __repr__(self):
            status = f"P{self.process_id}" if self.process_id else "FREE"
            return f"[{self.start}-{self.start+self.size}] {status} ({self.size}KB)"

def best_fit_allocate(blocks, process_id, process_size):
    """Allocate memory using Best Fit strategy"""
    best_idx = -1
    best_fit_size = float('inf')
    
    # Find the smallest block that fits
    for i, block in enumerate(blocks):
        if block.process_id is None and block.size >= process_size:
            if block.size < best_fit_size:
                best_fit_size = block.size
                best_idx = i
    
    if best_idx == -1:
        return None  # No suitable block
    
    block = blocks[best_idx]
    
    if block.size == process_size:
        block.process_id = process_id
    else:
        # Split the block
        new_block = MemoryBlock(
            start=block.start + process_size,
            size=block.size - process_size
        )
        block.size = process_size
        block.process_id = process_id
        blocks.insert(best_idx + 1, new_block)
    
    return block.start

# Example usage
memory_blocks = [
    MemoryBlock(0, 100),
    MemoryBlock(100, 200),
    MemoryBlock(300, 150),
    MemoryBlock(450, 250)
]

# Allocate process 1 (80KB) - goes to 100KB block
print(best_fit_allocate(memory_blocks, 1, 80))  # Returns 100
# Allocate process 2 (120KB) - goes to 150KB block
print(best_fit_allocate(memory_blocks, 2, 120))  # Returns 300
examples.explanation: Best Fit finds the smallest free block that can accommodate the process. This minimizes wasted space but requires scanning all free blocks. The algorithm splits blocks when they're larger than needed, creating new free blocks for remaining space.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - must search all free blocks

examples.title: Memory Compaction Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to perform memory compaction by moving all processes together
examples.code:
// Memory Compaction Algorithm
// blocks: list of all memory blocks (processes and free)

FUNCTION MemoryCompaction(blocks)
  // Sort blocks by starting address
  SORT blocks BY start ASCENDING
  
  currentAddress = 0
  newFreeList = []
  
  FOR each block IN blocks
    IF block.type == "PROCESS" THEN
      // Move process to new location
      IF block.start != currentAddress THEN
        // Copy process data to new location
        MoveProcess(block, currentAddress)
        // Update process base register
        UpdateBaseRegister(block.processId, currentAddress)
      END IF
      currentAddress = currentAddress + block.size
    ELSE
      // Record free block
      ADD {start: currentAddress, size: block.size} TO newFreeList
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // Coalesce adjacent free blocks
  RETURN CoalesceFreeBlocks(newFreeList)
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION CoalesceFreeBlocks(freeList)
  IF freeList.length < 2 THEN
    RETURN freeList
  END IF
  
  SORT freeList BY start ASCENDING
  coalesced = [freeList[0]]
  
  FOR i = 1 TO freeList.length - 1
    last = coalesced[LAST]
    current = freeList[i]
    
    IF last.start + last.size == current.start THEN
      // Adjacent free blocks - merge them
      last.size = last.size + current.size
    ELSE
      ADD current TO coalesced
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  RETURN coalesced
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Memory compaction moves all processes in memory to one end, consolidating all free space into a single contiguous block. This eliminates external fragmentation but requires significant CPU time and the ability to relocate processes (updating base registers).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for moving processes + O(m log m) for sorting free blocks
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) for new free list where m is number of free blocks

examples.title: Bounds Register Implementation
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating memory protection using base and limit registers
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates memory protection in contiguous allocation using bounds registers. The base register holds the starting address (relocation) and the limit register holds the size of the allocated region. Every memory access is checked against these bounds before being converted to a physical address.
